North-790; Hild-277; SCBI Cop-309 (plate coin); Bruun-87. Weight: 1.01 gms. Moneyer: Swertinc. The name of Derby

body_excerpt:

body_excerpt:

the Holy Dove in an interesting style with the Dove resembling a Roman legionary eagle. The Agnus Dei is holding a 
Bible between its forelegs and carries an inexplicable figure on its back.

lovely, dark patina. Extremely rare, early bracteate giving the full name and title of the king: +KANVT REX DAN. 
Following the death of Erik Lamb, Denmark was thrown into a civil war between three pretenders to the throne, all descend-
ing from different Danish kings: Canute, the grandson of Niels, Svend, the son of Erik Emune, and Valdemar, the grandson 
of Erik Ejegod.

meta_line: DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1518. Malmö Mint. Christian II. NGC VF-30. Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-9; Schou-2;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1518. Malmö Mint. Christian II. NGC VF-30. Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-9; Schou-2; 
Bruun-3931. Weight: 28.88 gms. Mintmaster: Dines Blicher. An utterly captivating example with a lovely, old cabinet 
toning. Of the utmost rarity with only a handful of examples known. Struck from dies made for the Gold Noble (Fr-6) 
which is today only known in the Danish National Museum.
The design was copied from the Nobles of king Hans but with a new reverse legend taken from the Liber Sapientiae 9:7: XPS 
IHS ELEGIT ME REGEM POPVLO SVO (“Christ Jesus chose me to govern his people”)

meta_line: DENMARK. Skilling / 10 Hvide, 1523. Roskilde or Serridslev Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU-55. Galster-51; Sieg-22.3;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Skilling / 10 Hvide, 1523. Roskilde or Serridslev Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU-55. Galster-51; Sieg-22.3; 
Jensen & Skjoldager-L-01/L-34; Schou-3; Bruun-3957. Weight: 3.97 gms. Mintmaster: Berndt Buske. A splendid example 
with a remarkable luster and superior details. Very rare variant with date on both sides.
This interesting issue was struck during the siege of Copenhagen at a military camp either in Roskilde or the village of Ser-
ridslev. The reverse bears the Latin inscription: DEO DVCE MILITES TRUMPHANT (‘With God’s leadership the troops will 
be victorious”).

meta_line: DENMARK. Skilling, 1524. Ribe Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU Details—Environmental Damage. Galster-70; Sieg-21;

Jensen & Skjoldager-S-01/S-05; Schou-4; Bruun-3964. Weight: 2.17 gms. Exceptionally well-preserved and presumably

Whereas the Danish Arms with the three lions have been chosen for the reverse just like the preceding Skillings from Christian 
II and Hans, the armored figure of the king is shown with the Holsatian nettle between his legs.

meta_line: DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1525. Visby Mint. Christian II.
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1525. Visby Mint. Christian II. 
NGC MS-61. Galster-143; Sieg-12; Llt-3; Delzanno-79; Schou-2; 
Bruun-4114. Weight: 4.24 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Seyer. Excep-
tionally well-preserved with most of the original silvering intact. 
Rare Siege type struck for the admiral and governor of Gotland, 
Søren Norby during the war between Christian II and his uncle, 
Frederik I. 
Norby’s use of the Danish Coat of Arms with the three lions leaves 
no doubt that he saw the island of Gotland as an integrated part 
of Denmark. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: DENMARK. Hvid, 1535. Copenhagen Mint. Christian II.
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Hvid, 1535. Copenhagen Mint. Christian II. 
NGC VF-30. Galster-87; Sieg-1; Schou-38; Bruun-4147. Weight: 
0.41 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. Excessively rare and histori-
cally interesting one-year type struck for Christian II during the 
so-called Count’s Feud, a war of succession that followed the 
death of Frederik I. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: O. T. Thomsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1880) Lot # 602.

meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1535). Malmø or Copenhagen
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1535). Malmø or Copenhagen 
Mint. Christian II. NGC MS-62. Galster-91; Sieg-5; Schou-3; 
Bruun-4149. Weight: 3.56 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. Struck 
in the name of the German Count, Christopher of Oldenburg 
(1504 - 1566) who gave name to the Count’s feud. Christopher 
sided with the former king, Christian II in the war against Chris-
tian (III) and acted as regent in the eastern Denmark between 
1534 and 1536. A very difficult type to obtain in any condition. 
€700-€1,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922)

meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Skilling (klippe), 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 8 Skilling (klippe), 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian 
III. NGC AU-55. Galster-93; Sieg-12; Schou-239; Bruun-4156. 
Weight: 2.72 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. Sublimely struck 
example, very rare thus.
This unusual klippe was issued as payment for the mercenary 
troops hired by Christian (III) during the war of succession be-
tween Christian (III) and his cousin Christian II. It was struck in 
Aarhus, where the former mintmaster of Gottorp, Reynold Junge 
had established a new mint, as the elected king needed one within 
the kingdom itself. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection

meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III.
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III. 
NGC Unc Details—Obverse Scratched. Galster-98; Sieg-13; 
Schou-28; Bruun-4194. Weight: 8.07 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold 
Junge. Although somewhat double-struck, the present example 
is vastly superior to what is normally seen at auctions. An unusu-
ally nice portrait, struck on a sound flan with considerable luster 
remaining.
During the Count’s Feud, the Mint had to move with the army, and 
in August 1535 Reynold Junge established a new Mint at the royal 
court in Roskilde. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

body_excerpt:

body_excerpt:

original minting of only 16 examples this is almost a miraculous find, and it is today believed to be unique in private 
hands with the only other known example resting at the National Museum. 
The extremely low mintage was due to the fact that the king opposed the minting of Speciedalers as it was simply not profit-
able. The rather common 1563 Speciedaler (bearing the dat

body_excerpt:

Schou-10; Bruun-4451. Weight: 9.47 gms. Mintmaster: Poul

been on the market (from the Hede, Lerche and Bonde collec -

These primitive and base coins, which were minted during the

meta_line: DENMARK. , 1572. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. , 1572. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-
50. Dav-8229; Hede-17; Sieg-22; Schou-10; Aagaard & Märch -
er-72.1; Bruun-4525. Weight: 29.07 gms. Mintmaster: Hans 
Delhusen. Engraver: Cort Dellhusen. Very nice example struck 
on an unusually fine flan and boasting a charming, iridescent

of Sweden in the Danish royal arms, was struck from silver paid by

ent example of this highly interesting type, which is presumed unique in private hands, was in the Royal Danish 
Collection in the mid 19th cent. before it was traded with Count Bille-Brahe. 
This type is from the first series struck at the newly opened Mint in the Frederiksborg Deer Garden northwest of 
Copenhagen. This was the first time roller dies were used in Denmark.

Exceptionally well-struck on an even flan. With an outstanding issue as the present it is by no means surprising that 
there is evidence of a contemporary mounting positioned right above the royal coat of arms presumably to be used as 
a medallion by high ranking dignitaries. In fact, most of the surviving Haderslev gold issues have traces of mounting. 
According to the most recent

body_excerpt:

un-4625. Weight: 9.57 gms. Mintmaster: Gert van Campen. Very few examples are known in private hands of which

meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Harck-6.23.2;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Harck-6.23.2; 
Schou-8; Bruun-4650. Weight: 3.20 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe. A truly magnifi-
cent example with normally lost details and a blazing luster beneath an alluring iridescent toning. Scarce variant without 
curlicue below the elephant.
This beautiful and amusing type was according to tradition struck as largesse money to be used in connection with the cel-
ebration of the Hanseatic city of Hamburg swearing allegiance to the Danish king on 30 October 1603.

meta_line: DENMARK. 6 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-45; KM-26; Hede-11; Sieg-147;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 6 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-45; KM-26; Hede-11; Sieg-147; 
Schou-3; Bruun-4668. Weight: 13.05 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A very nice example with all four corners 
sharply struck. Of the originally 143 examples minted few still exist.
Two different obverse dies were used with markedly different portraits of which this, resembling the one used for minor 
coins like the Soesling, Hede-82, is by far the rarest. At the time the value of 6 Daler corresponded to 3.5 Ducats (Hungarian 
Guldens).
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1612. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-48; KM-51;
body_excerpt:

Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; Schou-2; Bruun-4865. Weight: 8.93 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Delicate purplish hues 
further enhances the incredible eye-appeal of this magnificent piece boasting a splendid luster and wonderful details. 
The inspiration for this beautiful, amusing and very rare type came from the British Rose Nobles that were widely used 
in international trade. In the contemporary official accounts the type is referred to as a “Guldridder” from the Dutch, 
“Gouden rijder” .

body_excerpt:

Taler with a remarkable luster and superlative details. The beautiful type with the curved, so-called “German” provincial 
shields is very rare.
In an entry of March 31st 1624 the king wrote in one of his diaries that he had received 140 Speciedalers from Schwabe 
which he had struck from Norwegian silver. Even though there might have been silver from other sources in the alloy ther

Hede-60B; Sieg-104.2; Harck-23.2; Schou-17; Bruun-5371. Weight:28.79 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A few 
minor obverse surface marks aside, this is an absolutely gorgeous example with a choice striking and a remarkable lus-
ter. The present specimen of this beautiful Taler type is with all likelihood unique as no other examples neither publicly 
held or in private collections have been recorded. 
The issue should probably be considered a pattern that was realized as the crown on the rev

meta_line: DENMARK. 20 Skilling, 1644. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. KM-PnA8; Hede-146;
body_excerpt:

Sieg-72.2; Schou-58; Aagaard-KP1; Bruun-5697. Weight: 8.18 gms. Clearly struck and nicely re-toning following a 
gentle, old cleaning. Excessively rare and highly interesting. 
This pattern was never used for circulation for at least two reasons: The Hebrew tetragrammaton legend rendered the V 
incorrectly, and although the value would be on a par with the old Rigsmark, the denomination of 20 in a duodecimal 
system would be impractical.
€6,000-€8,000

meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Chris -
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Chris -
tian IV . NGC Unc Details—Damaged, Cleaned. Fr-54; KM-
74.2; Hede-25E; Sieg-134.5; Schou-3; Bruun-5829. Weight: 5.91 
gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Five very small drill-holes 
are not easily explicable but might just have been done to test the 
gold. Otherwise a most attractive example perfectly struck on an 
even flan. Christian IV died early in 1648 (February 28th), and 
the present very rare Double Gold Krone is the only one minted 
by Heinrich Köhler.

DENMARK. 4 Ducats, 1663-IS. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-116; KM-unlisted; Hede-61; Sieg-

han Stichmann. Engraver: Johan Blum. A flawless, shimmery Mint State example of this beautiful and literally majestic 
type. The only other example recorded, now in the National Museum of Denmark, was sold in the 1925 auction of 
Bruun duplicates as lot 2563.
The engraver Johan Blum (ca. 1599 - 1669) had been working as an engraver at the Mint in Bremen. In 1657 he was ap-

meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Ducat, 1664. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Bent. Fr-102; KM-229; Hede-15B;

Sieg-106.2; Schou-11; Aagaard-42.1; Bruun-6418. Weight: 1.72 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Herbach. Engravers: Matthias

Enchanting Ducat of the Utmost Rarity

meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1665. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-61. Cf. Fr-101; KM-265.1; Hede-24A; Sieg-
body_excerpt:

123.1; Schou-8; Aagaard-47.1; Bruun-6439. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Caspar Herbach. Engraver: Jer -

90.1; Schou-16; Aagaard-117.1; Bruun-6576. Weight: 29.01 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Although the engraver of

meta_line: DENMARK. 1/4 Ducat (3 Mark), 1668. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-129; KM-264.1; Hede-40A;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/4 Ducat (3 Mark), 1668. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-129; KM-264.1; Hede-40A; 
Sieg-102.3; Schou-6; Aagaard-51.2; Bruun-6641. Weight: 0.96 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Caspar Herbach. Engraver: Jer-
emias Hercules. A scarce date of this unusual type which quite unconventionally for a gold coin bears the denomination 
of 3 Mark (1/2 Taler) written all across the reverse.
It has been suggested that these very small gold coins were minted for the king to be used as handouts on his domestic travel.

Schou-45; Aagaard-142.1; Bruun-6712. Weight: 2.93 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger (?). A Select Uncirculated piece

from the same year (Hede-37) might indicate that the variant without mintmark and a somewhat larger diameter was in

meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Speciedaler, ND (1669). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. KM-Pn19; Hede-36; Sieg-
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/2 Speciedaler, ND (1669). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. KM-Pn19; Hede-36; Sieg-
141; Schou-19; Aagaard-63.1; Bruun-6746. Weight: 14.50 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Fine old cabinet toning. 
Extremely rare and enigmatic issue. It was struck with dies made for the 10 Ducat 1669 and might (together with the 
very few other examples of different weights, Schou-19a) be considered an off-strike in silver. The weight of the present 
singular example (Schou-19) is however consistent with what would be expected from a Half Taler and is by KM listed 
as a pattern. 
€3,000-€5,0

Bruun-6777. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Engraver: Jeremias Hercules. Eminently lustrous and with

meta_line: NORW AY . 2 Ducats, 1673. Christiania mint. Christian V . NGC MS-61. Fr-12d; KM-123; Hede-16A; Sieg-74.1;
body_excerpt:
NORW AY . 2 Ducats, 1673. Christiania mint. Christian V . NGC MS-61. Fr-12d; KM-123; Hede-16A; Sieg-74.1; 
NMD-14A; Schou-8; Bruun-6811. Weight: 7.00 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Magnificently lustrous and far su-
perior to the few other examples that have appeared in the market since the days of L. E. Bruun.
This beautiful type was presumably struck at the behest of Norwegian Governor-general Ulrik Frederik Gyldenløve. He was 
the illegitimate son of Frederik III and resided permanently in Norway from June 1673 to November 1679.
€30,000-€40,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

body_excerpt:

body_excerpt:

8A; Schou-2; Bruun-6884. Weight: 9.82 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Y ounger. A very pleasing example of this

this almost mythological rarity was believed to have been struck in Copenhagen but both the style of the portrait and

meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1691. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-58. Fr-156; KM-415.1; Hede-31A; Sieg-104.1;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1691. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-58. Fr-156; KM-415.1; Hede-31A; Sieg-104.1; 
Schou-7; Bruun-7061. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Elder. Light golden toning and consider-

meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1677-1678). Kristianstad (Siege
body_excerpt:

coinage). Christian V . NGC MS-63. KM-cf. 7 (copper); Hede-
134B; Sieg-148.2; Schou-55; Aagaard-4S-1; Bruun-7280. Weight:

meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1802. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1802. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1; 
FP-47.4; Bruun-7974. Weight: 3.49 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Valentin Bauert. A remarkable ex-
ample with an excellent luster.
The so-called “Wildman Ducat” was the last Danish Ducat type produced with the 1802 being the very last date of issue. 
This type is the only Danish coin which gives the exact weight and fineness in the design, suiting it for international trade. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: NORW AY . 1/4 Penny (?), ND (1177-1202). Bergen Mint (?). Sverre Sigurdsson. NGC AU-53. Schive-VI:90; NM-18:1
body_excerpt:

(1976); NM-8 (1992); Bruun-9063; Weight: 0.06 gms. Hairline split in flan but nicely centered, toned and with excellent 
detail. 
Almost all of the Bracteates minted in the latter part of the 12th century were of the so-called “lettered” type, and the few ex-
ceptions, like the present with a crowned, facing head are extremely rare. This type seems to have struck over a longer period 
of time with varying weights, and the denomination is uncertain.

meta_line: NORW AY . Penny, ND (1285-90). Oslo Mint. Håkon (V) Magnusson as Duke. NGC EF-40. Schive-X:5; NM-32:1
body_excerpt:

(1976); NM-1 (1992); NMH-240; Bruun-9186; Weight: 1.37 gms. A very rare coin with an exquisite, dark patina and 
sharp details.
Håkon, king Eirik’s younger brother, ruled areas around Oslo and Stavanger as a duke from 1280 to 1299, with the right to 
issue coins of his own. After taking over the throne in 1299, Oslo gradually took over the functions of capital from Bergen. 
On this especially interesting variant, Haakon is wearing a Ducal crown in the shape of a wreath of roses.

meta_line: NORW AY . Penny, ND (1299-1300). Oslo Mint (?). Håkon V Magnusson. NGC AU-55. Schive-X:23; NM-36 (1976);
body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1299-1300). Oslo Mint (?). Håkon V Magnusson. NGC AU-55. Schive-X:23; NM-36 (1976); 
NM-1 (1992); NMH-246; Bruun-9196; Weight: 0.73 gms. Rare and exceptionally well-struck with unusually pleasing 
surfaces.
This type which was formerly believed to be a 1/2 Penny is known through the archives as a “ Albi Rosati” (White Roseate) 
that although of a lighter weight was struck with an increased fineness as a counterpart to the vehemently unpopular so-
called “Black Crown” Pennies. The design was copied from the Scottish Sterlings.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: NORW AY . Penny, ND (1380-87). Oslo Mint (?). Olav IV Håkonsson. NGC AU-50. Schive-XII:51; NM-51 (1976);
body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1380-87). Oslo Mint (?). Olav IV Håkonsson. NGC AU-50. Schive-XII:51; NM-51 (1976); 
NM-1 (1992); NMH-277; Bruun-9217; Weight: 0.20 gms. Old numbering in ink at lower obverse. Rare and of excep-
tional condition for type.
This highly interesting bracteate type with a crowned O, was struck for the underaged Olav by his mother, Queen Regent 
Margrethe I. No coins are known to have been struck for Olav (Oluf II) in Denmark. Olav IV was the last Norwegian king 
to be born on Norwegian soil until Harald V who took over the throne in 1991.
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Coll

meta_line: NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1483-1505). Nidaros (Trondheim)
body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1483-1505). Nidaros (Trondheim) 
Mint. Hans. NGC EF-40. Galster-157; Sieg-3; NMD-2; Schive-
XIV:26; Schou-215; Bruun-9242. Weight: 0.68 gms. A splendid 
example with excellent details and of unusually sound metal. 
Extremely rare type issued by the Archbishop of Nidaros, Gaute 
Ivarsson but struck in the name of king Hans. 
On this particular die variant the engraver has by mistake placed 
a C between the O and the L in the name of the patron saint of 
Nidaros, OLA VVS (Olaf the Holy). 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x

meta_line: NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1505-10). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint.
body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1505-10). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. 
Gaute Ivarsson. NGC EF-45. Galster-159; Sieg-5; NMD-11; 
Schive-XIV:32-33; Schou-220; Bruun-9243. Weight: 0.80 gms. 
Rare this nice.
Gaute Ivarsson, who was the archbishop of Nidaros from 1475 
until his death in 1510, managed to reinforce the authority of the 
Nidaros archbishopric partly due to having set up a Mint in his 
palace.  
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x

body_excerpt:

6; NMD-1; Schou-11; Schive-XVIII:5; Bruun-9261. Weight: 2.32 gms. Mintmaster: Fully lustrous, brilliantly struck 
and highly detailed. A “must be seen to be believed” survivor of unsurpassable beauty. Of the utmost rarity with only 
6 examples recorded of which this is the best. The other five are all in public collections: two in Denmark (National 
Museum), 2 in Norway (Bergen and Oslo) and one in Sweden (Royal Coin Cabinet).
Remarkably, the 6 known examples have been struck from five

meta_line: NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1510-22). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. Erik Valkendorf. NGC AU-58. Galster-184; Sieg-5;
body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1510-22). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. Erik Valkendorf. NGC AU-58. Galster-184; Sieg-5; 
NMD-6; Schive-XVIII:13; Schou-14; Bruun-9262. Weight: 0.64 gms. A highly desirable piece, astoundingly well-struck 
and with traces of original luster. Among the late medieval Norwegian types, the Hvid from Erik Valkendorf has always 
stood out as something completely unobtainable. With only two other examples recorded, both in public collections in 
Copenhagen and Bergen, it is almost a miracle that the present example is not just there but actually wonderfully well 
preserved.

body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Skilling, ND (1524-33). Bergen Mint. Frederik I. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Galster-170; Sieg-2; NMD-7; 
Schou-22; Jensen & Skjoldager-N-07; Bruun-9275. Weight: 1.88 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Hansen. A lovely example with 
incredible details and very rare thus.
This interesting emission was overseen by Vincens Lunge (1486-1537) who early in the reign of Frederik I was sent to North-
ern Norway to enforce the reign of the new king. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and gold 
coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt

Hede-33; Sieg-47; NMD-69B; Schou-14; Bruun-10114. Weight: 14.44 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Engraver: Jo-
nas Olufsen Lunder. Extremely rare and attractive 1/2 Speciedaler of the so-called “large head” variant. From the whole 
reign of Christian V , 1/2 Speciedalers were only minted in four years (1671, 1673, 1674 and 1693), the first three that 
were minted at Christiania are all exceedingly rare and highly coveted by all collectors of Norwegian 17th century coins.

meta_line: NORW AY . Gold Off-metal strike Mark, 1685. Christiania Mint. Christian V . NGC VF-35. Fr-Unlisted; KM-cf. Pn30
body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Gold Off-metal strike Mark, 1685. Christiania Mint. Christian V . NGC VF-35. Fr-Unlisted; KM-cf. Pn30 
(silver); Hede-18; Sieg-80; JS-N2190; Schou-1; Bruun-10272. Weight: 5.19 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Y ounger. 
The only other example in existence, weighing 7.20 gms. is in the Danish National Museum. The varying weights sug-
gest that it is not a regular gold coin but rather an off-strike of an unknown Mark from the so-called “Dannebrog-series” . 
A both interesting and mysterious piece that will be a spectacular addition to any collection of Norwegian coins.
€15,000-€20,000

NORDSKE FIELDE SLIGE FRUGTER HAR I V ÆLDE. (“The 
Danish king’s Norwegian cliffs abound with such fruits”). A very 
handsome piece with clear details and a subtle re-toning after

very rare and sought after.

meta_line: SWEDEN. Penny, ND (1022-50). Sigtuna Mint. Anund Jakob. NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Llt-7; Delzanno-104;
body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Penny, ND (1022-50). Sigtuna Mint. Anund Jakob. NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Llt-7; Delzanno-104; 
Malmer-14.310.1252; SMH-612; Bruun-11151. Weight: 1.43 gms. Moneyer: Thormodt. An old, dark toning covers the 
coin and boosts the already clear and well-struck devices. A very rare type with fully legible legends. The coin imitates 
Cnut’s Pointed Helmet coinage which is conventionally dated to the years between 1024 and 1030.
Of the two almost identical reverse dies from the moneyer Thormoth, Malmer knew of 28 coins (14 of each), with eight in 
private collections.
Son of Olof Skötkonung

body_excerpt:

olf. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Dav-4520; KM-143; SM-36b; 
Delzanno-24; Hagander-cf. 81; Bruun-11850. Weight: 28.87 
gms. Mintmaster: Markus Kock. Very rare and excellently toned 
example; the concavity of the strike means the obverse appears 
worn but that is for the most part in the strike; the reverse is 
lustrous and beautiful.
The minting of silver coins stopped in 1626 in Stockholm and was 
resumed in Sala in 1631. In 1632 the mint moved back to Stock -
holm, but all silver coins dated 1631 are struck in Sala.

mintmark. Light waviness with no stress marks to the metal, 
hints of luster playing in the legend and a sharp strike with very 
little wear.
Augsburg in Southern Bavaria was conquered by Swedish forces in 
1632 and held till 1634, when the Imperial army retook it. Augs-
burg coins in the name of Gustav II Adolf were minted in 1632, 
1633, and 1634 (the two last

lian Peace of 1648. It remained Swedish till 1720, when it was again ceded to the Elector of Hannover, Georg (George I of 
Great Britain) at the end of the Great Nordic War.

body_excerpt:

memorating these restored rights.

KM-PM46; SM-169; Delzanno-1721; Tingström-B2; Bruun-13822. Weight: 1491 gms.
A number ‘7’ is written in red chalk.
A sticker with the hand-written number ‘1307’ is placed on the plate. 
In 1718 a depreciation of the plate coinage took place, making their new value 2/3 of the old. The coins in the possession of 
the Crown or the banks were exempt, however, and were fitted with the countermark of the arms of Götaland to show it.  
€900-€1,100
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship

meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 1/2 Talers, ND (1607-16). Johann Adolf. NGC AU-55. Dav-unlisted, cf.
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 1/2 Talers, ND (1607-16). Johann Adolf. NGC AU-55. Dav-unlisted, cf. 
LS444; KM-unlisted, cf. 3; Sieg-12.4; Lange-cf. 264D; Bruun-14147. Weight: 73.07 gms. Excessively rare, unlisted as 2.5 
Taler in KM, Lange and Davenport. This spectacular and impressive 2.5 Taler is only listed in Sieg, who were aware of 
the L. E. Bruun Collection. The closest we can come to anything like this coin is a 1.5 Taler from the same dies, which 
was sold in 2021. The present coin has wonderful, original surfaces and plenty of luster.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L.E. Bruun Col

tremely rare single-year Taler issue. Lustrous with some smoothing in obverse fields; the reverse uncirculated with 
beautiful, subtle toning; exquisite detailing throughout. Only one other example is listed as sold (twice) on CoinAr -
chives, this present example being in far better condition.
The shield on the reverse holds the arms of Norway, Schleswig, Holstein, Stormarn, and Dit

body_excerpt:

Schleswig Mint. Christian Albrecht. NGC EF Details—
Cleaned. KM-133; Sieg-15; Lange-404; Bruun-14435. Weight:

meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg. Taler,
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg. Taler, 
1627//1626. Reinfeldt or Sonderburg Mint. Alexander. NGC 
VF Details—Cleaned. Dav-3717; KM-28; Sieg-5; Lange-560; 
Bruun-14678. Weight: 27.26 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Lilien-
thal. Very rare Taler struck on the death of Alexander in 1627. 
Unevenly toned but gleaming with multicolor. An interesting 
zwitter/mule in that the reverse die of the 1626 Taler was reused 
on this coin, giving it two different dates.
The Norwegian lion sits atop the reverse arms as the dukes bore the 
hereditary title of ‘heir to Norway’ or ‘Heres Norvegie’. 
€3,000-€4,0

ler, 1615. Altona Mint. Ernst III. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned.

meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Schaumburg-Pinneberg.
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Schaumburg-Pinneberg. 
1/2 Taler, ND (1601-22). Altona Mint. Ernst III. NGC AU-53. 
KM-102; Lange cf. 920; Weinmeister-159; Bruun-14918. Weight: 
14.27 gms. Extremely rare Half Taler in extraordinary condition. 
An old, dark patina covers the surfaces and gives the coin, along 
with the colorful toning, a most sublime expression. Light wear 
to the high points but well-struck and with excellent details.
Portrait coins from Ernst III are all very rare; the sublime portrait 
and spectacular condition of this coin makes it a true unicorn. 
€1,000-€1,400
From the L.E.

stadt Mint. Christian August. NGC AU-53. Dav-623; KM-78.1; 
Lange-511A; Bruun-15039. Weight: 17.43 gms. Mintmaster: An-
dreas Woltereck. A very rare 2 Mark or Gulden as seen by the 
lack of a photo or even a drawing of it in Davenport. The surfaces 
are uncleaned with a greenish tint hiding promises of luster, and 
while there is a softness to the top points in the portrait it seems 
to be a weakness in the strike rather than wear or possibly a com-
bination.
Christian August of Holstein-Gottorp-Eutin was the grandson on 
his mother’s sid

meta_line: GERMANY . Lübeck (Bishopric). 2 Mark, 1723-AW . Glück -
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Lübeck (Bishopric). 2 Mark, 1723-AW . Glück -
stadt Mint. Christian August. NGC MS-62. Dav-623; KM-78.2; 
Lange-cf. 511A; Bruun-15040. Weight: 17.10 gms. Mintmaster: 
Andreas Woltereck. A very rare 2 Mark or Gulden in extraordi-
nary condition; old, greenish tinted toning covers the uncircu -
lated surfaces with luster shimmering underneath. A die crack 
runs through the portrait. The elegant details in the hair shows 
off not only the artistry of the die cutter but also the condition of 
the piece, as there are no flattened part, neither from weak strike 
nor wear.
No recorded sales

meta_line: GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1775. Altona Mint. Friedrich August. NGC AU-58. Dav-2411;
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1775. Altona Mint. Friedrich August. NGC AU-58. Dav-2411; 
KM-85; Trippler/Kalvelage-371; Lange 523; Bruun-15140. Weight: 28.89 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Heinrich Lyng. Excel-
lent condition for type with playful luster around the devices and attractive, old toning; only 1,000 pieces struck.
Struck to Danish standard in 1777 re-using the reverse of the Albertstaler of 1775, the obverse bears the title ‘DVX REGN 
OLD’ - ‘Duke Regent of Oldenburg’, a title he was only granted in 1776.
Friedrich August only had three coins struck: the 1776 gold Pistol
